In this article, we explore the impact of VISualization on the six inches between your ears. We interviewed VIS Expert Cali Werner, LCSW, who shares insights on the neuroscience behind visualization. She emphasizes its role in enhancing performance, building muscle memory, and fostering resilience in the face of challenges.

![](//images.ctfassets.net/xqwrisc54mjr/5mWKw3qhKed8Cyk8fJ9UAK/bc236edb4982af6a360e8b41fa50db2b/photo.jpeg?w=120&h=120&fm=jpg&q=100)
![](//images.ctfassets.net/xqwrisc54mjr/54XsBjMsF3mnTiUFNXORgh/959f853ab38b297fa277749b24c046ca/Cali_Outside.jpg?w=120&h=120&fm=jpg&q=100)

By: Haneyya Siddiqui

VIS Creator

![](/assets/user_creator-62d83e91770f770f564e33c2a2b8b59e9c2083db04f1fd931d93289c41bd572e.svg)

& Cali Werner

VIS Expert

![](/assets/user_expert-c57394dbf5ea15557d4e07c2f2a500f4237608759ec5e462cdf076d844dcb75b.svg)

Topic: Mind

February 12 2025

![Becoming an Artist of Your Sport (Desktop Wallpaper)-2](//images.ctfassets.net/xqwrisc54mjr/5dTE5ibe3oDMtEJ4RlMkhA/f70b0bfcf9a4e99769e42ec1b81fe3fb/Becoming_an_Artist_of_Your_Sport__Desktop_Wallpaper_-2.jpg?fm=jpg&q=100)
![Becoming an Artist of Your Sport (Desktop Wallpaper)-2](//images.ctfassets.net/xqwrisc54mjr/5dTE5ibe3oDMtEJ4RlMkhA/f70b0bfcf9a4e99769e42ec1b81fe3fb/Becoming_an_Artist_of_Your_Sport__Desktop_Wallpaper_-2.jpg?fm=jpg&q=100)

Ancient Chinese philosopher Lao Tzu once said, “Watch your thoughts; they become your words; watch your words; they become your actions.” His quote echoes the science behind a critical performance tool used by many athletes today: Visualization. More than a mental exercise, visualization taps into brain functions that mimic the effects of physical practice, strengthening neural pathways and enhancing muscle memory.

VIS Expert and Licensed Clinical Social Worker, Cali Werner, explains, “When you visualize something, the same neural pathways are engaged in the brain as when you actually perform the action.” This illustrates how our thoughts can directly impact our physical capabilities.

**Neuroplasticity in Play**

Visualization, or mental imagery, operates on a principle known as neuroplasticity. This phenomenon allows the brain to reorganize itself by forming new connections in response to experiences—both real and imagined. “Visualization allows for neuroplasticity to take place; it strengthens connections of motor and cognitive pathways,” Werner notes, emphasizing how repeated mental rehearsal can create lasting changes in the brain. “The more you visualize, the stronger those connections become, leading to improved performance,” she adds.

**The Brain...Activated!**

The occipital lobe, located at the back of the brain, is crucial for processing visual information. When we visualize, this area becomes highly active and we can create mental images even without direct visual stimuli. A simple [example](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/gifted-ed-guru/201211/brain-basics-part-one-the-power-visualization) is when you’re reading descriptive text, and the brain thinks about certain  images based on the words.

The parts of temporal lobes like the [amygdala](https://envision.app/visualization/the-science-of-visualization/) (a small almond-shaped structure in the brain) are involved in memory storage and retrieval, playing a different, significant role. When we visualize a scenario, we often rely on past experiences stored in our memory, accessing both the sensory details and emotional contexts associated with those memories. This enhances the quality and effectiveness of the mental imagery that we are processing.

“Consistency in visualization practice can be just as important as physical training.”

Cali Werner, LCSW, VIS Expert and social worker specializing in OCD and anxiety

![](/assets/icons/twitter-alt-d11a7ebafc027f11774fbad29e57f5cfdbac3b89b80d720bd96498bb79b96246.svg)
![](/assets/share-copy-link-1e5684477608557350005b1bc8f0eeee778b0b5239f7bff56b3331b00a3286c1.svg)

The frontal lobe, responsible for higher-order functions such as decision-making and critical thinking, also comes into play. It helps direct our attention and control the cognitive processes necessary to maintain focus on the visualization task. The brain's remarkable ability to create and manipulate mental images, which can be harnessed in educational and therapeutic settings, improves muscle memory. In turn, physical actions feel smoother and more automatic when we train and compete.

**Primed For Pressure**

Werner highlights the benefit of mentally rehearsing both positive and challenging scenarios: “Visualizing both scenarios helps athletes to be on top of their game regardless of outcomes.” By doing so, athletes can prepare not only for success but also for unexpected difficulties, boosting resilience. “Preparing for challenges mentally can help athletes respond better when faced with adversity,” she adds.

**Broader Benefits**

[Research](https://discobolulunefs.ro/media/September2020.4.pdf) shows that visualization involves accessing an altered state of consciousness – biochemistry and brain waves modify, and the participant becomes able to speed up healing and performance. For athletes, mental imagery also promotes a state of flow, enhances confidence, and reduces performance anxiety. According to Werner, visualization “often leads to a zone of flow” where performance feels almost effortless, pushing athletes to perform at their best. “When you’re in that state, everything feels right. You’re completely in tune with your body and your environment,” she says.

**VISualize, VISualize, VISualize!**

Establishing a consistent visualization routine is crucial. “Just like you train to build other muscles in sport performance, your brain is a key muscle needed for sport performance,” Werner suggests. This highlights the need for athletes to integrate mental training into their routines. For athletes, regularly practicing mental imagery can yield long-term gains, building resilience, and muscle memory that lasts. “Consistency in visualization practice can be just as important as physical training,” she explains.

By understanding and applying these techniques, we can maximize our potential, preparing not only for the challenges of competition but also for the mental aspects that contribute to our success.

![](/assets/action-24bc649e9b28822f858beb3508062bfc4ae0232656f873895c358b004ad67db8.svg)

Take Action

Curious about how to practice VISualization? Check out our article [The Power Of VISualization](https://www.voiceinsport.com/post/power-of-visualization). Looking for more research-backed information about its benefits? Read [How VISualization Can Improve Your Game](https://voiceinsport.com/post/mind/2M3jLIV0qFUIpwtXEuoNg3). You can also schedule a Session with Werner to learn more about overcoming perfectionism by visiting her [VIS Bio](https://www.voiceinsport.com/cali-werner).

--- SOURCE 4: Science-Based Mental Training & Visualization for Improved Learning ---
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0RYyQRQFgFk

SUMMARY:
Science-Based Mental Training & Visualization for Improved Learning | Huberman Lab Podcast
Andrew Huberman
6910000 subscribers
20627 likes
950208 views
24 Apr 2023
In this episode, I explore the science of mental visualization and its application for learning motor and cognitive skills. I discuss neuroplasticity-based skill development and the roles of focus, sleep, movement restriction, and agitation. I then present five key principles of mental visualization to enhance learning speed, accuracy, and consistency. I also provide examples of specific protocols, including repetitions, rest periods, and session frequency, and how to adapt these methods for injuries or breaks from traditional training. Throughout, I reference the scientific studies supporting these concepts. This episode should allow anyone to learn or teach more effectively through the use of mental visualization and training.

#HubermanLab #Science #Neuroscience

# Science-Based Mental Training & Visualization for Improved Learning | Huberman Lab Podcast
## Andrew Huberman
6910000 subscribers
20627 likes

### Description
950208 views
Posted: 24 Apr 2023
In this episode, I explore the science of mental visualization and its application for learning motor and cognitive skills. I discuss neuroplasticity-based skill development and the roles of focus, sleep, movement restriction, and agitation. I then present five key principles of mental visualization to enhance learning speed, accuracy, and consistency. I also provide examples of specific protocols, including repetitions, rest periods, and session frequency, and how to adapt these methods for injuries or breaks from traditional training. Throughout, I reference the scientific studies supporting these concepts. This episode should allow anyone to learn or teach more effectively through the use of mental visualization and training.

### Transcript:
Mental Training & Visualization welcome to the huberman Lab podcast where we discuss science and science-based tools for everyday [Music] life I'm Andrew huberman and I'm a professor of neurobiology and Opthalmology at Stanford school of medicine today we are discussing mental training and visualization mental training and visualization is a fascinating process that has been shown over and over again in now hundreds of studies to improve our ability to learn anything when I say anything I mean the ability to learn music the ability to learn and perform mathematics the ability to learn and perform motor skills in sport in dance across essentially all domains the other incredible thing about mental training and visualization is that as you'll soon see when you go into the literature that is the scientific studies on mental training and visualization you quickly realize that it does not take a lot of mental training and visualization in order to get better at anything however that mental training and visualization has has to be performed in a very specific way and today we will discuss exactly how to do mental training and visualization in the specific ways that allow it to complement the actual performance of a motor or cognitive skill to allow you to learn more quickly and to consolidate that is to keep that information in mind and body so that you can perform those cognitive tasks music tasks motor tasks etc for long periods of time without ever forgetting how to do them all of mental training and visualization relies on what I consider consider really the Holy Grail of our brain and nervous system and that's neuroplasticity neuroplasticity is our nervous system which of course includes the brain the spinal cord and all the connections between the brain and spinal cord and the organs and tissues of the body and then all the neural connections back from the organs and tissues of the body to the brain and spinal cord so the whole thing in both directions has the ability to change in response to experience in ways that are adaptive that is that allows us to do things that we could not do before and by doing those things or by being able to perform those mental operations we can do better in the world that we live in we can perform new tasks we can think new thoughts we can come up with novel solutions to pre-existing problems that before really vexed us and that we couldn't overcome all of that is considered neuroplasticity so today what I'm going to cover is a brief summary of what neuroplasticity is that is how it occurs in the brain and body this is extremely important to understand if you're going to use Mental training and visualization then I'm going to talk about what happens in our brain and body when we do mental visualization in a dedicated way many people have heard perhaps that when you imagine something happening that your brain doesn't know the difference between that imagination of the thing happening and the real thing happening turns out that is not true it is simply not true however there is somewhat of an equivalence between a real experience and an imagined experience and we'll talk about the difference between those and how that can be leveraged in order to get the the most out of mental training and visualization then I will cover exactly which types of mental training and visualization work best across all domains meaning for Music Learning mathematics solving puzzles motor learning Sports Performance etc etc to really allow you a template in which you can plug in or designate what you're going to do each day for a brief period of time in order to accelerate your learning in whatever you choose and then I'm going to go into a bit of what happens in the brains of different types of people uh these different types of people that I'm referring to are people who have more or less of a natural ability to imagine things and visualize them because it turns out that we vary tremendously from one individual to the next in terms of our ability to mentally visualize and imagine things and our ability to get better at that over time and the good news is anyone can get better second component that's really required for self-directed adaptive plasticity and that's periods of deep rest in particular a goodn night sleep in particular on the night that follows that focused attention to the thing you're trying to learn there are now hundreds of studies in both animal models and in humans showing that it is really during sleep and other states of deep relaxation things involves this thing that we call long-term depression and that's because much of what is happening when we learn a new motor skill is that we are deped pressing or suppressing specific actions in order to generate a very specific coordinated action some of the best examples of long-term depression can actually be borrowed from developmental plasticity so for instance if you've ever sat across from an infant who is trying to eat their meal so imagine a one and a half-year-old or a 2-year-old trying to eat some noodles or some soup or any kind of baby suitable food with a spoon and they're holding the spoon or they're trying to hold the spoon what you'll notice is that their motor movements are terribly uncoordinated they often will take that spoon to their cheek or to their eye or to they lack sufficient numbers of neural connections synapses between neurons but rather because they have too many connections between too many different neurons the neural circuits that control very enriched_points.expanded_content: Visual representations of neural connections illustrate how synapses form and strengthen, showcasing neuroplasticity's dynamic nature and its role in learning and memory.
Learning engages neural pathways, activating long-term potentiation, which enhances synaptic connections, reinforcing memories and skills through repeated practice and visualization.

enriched_points.point_title: Importance of visual aids in understanding
enriched_points.expanded_content: Visual aids simplify complex concepts of neuroplasticity, making it easier to grasp the brain's adaptability and the impact of learning on neural structures.
Imagery stimulates emotional and cognitive responses, facilitating deeper engagement in learning processes and enhancing retention through vivid mental representations.
